Name: Bodong Chen
Comment: Considering our course's emphasis on collaborative knowledge building and collective cognitive responsibility, how might these 'unproductive' collaboration patterns hinder a group's ability to advance educational ideas? What are some strategies you've seen or used to mitigate 'free-rider effects' or superficial discussions?

Name: Bodong Chen
Comment: This framework offers a structured way to analyze scaffolding. As you continue reading, consider how these five questions can help us critically evaluate existing educational interventions or design new ones for collaborative learning contexts.

Name: Bodong Chen
Comment: Argumentation, peer feedback, and joint regulation are crucial for collaborative learning. How do these activities contribute to the 'active construction of shared understanding' and 'collective cognitive responsibility' that are central to our course goals?

Name: Bodong Chen
Comment: This section introduces different types of external scaffolds that mirror the internal script hierarchy. How might you use these four scaffold types (play, scene, scriptlet, role) to design a structured peer critique activity for your Idea Development Portfolio, ensuring clear guidance for each phase and role?

Name: Bodong Chen
Comment: This highlights a critical challenge in learning sciences research: how to reliably and validly assess internal scripts. Based on your understanding of research methodologies, what are some alternative or complementary approaches you might propose to measure these complex cognitive structures?

Name: Ariana Ravitch
Comment: I really have enjoyed experiences where students are encouraged to connect material to their lives, share this with their peers, and then create a discussion with these shared memories. I have also seen 'role' assignments, though I think this becomes unequal in distribution and lends itself to questionable social dynamics as well (unfortunately).

Name: Ariana Ravitch
Comment: This is interesting! I have heard 'scripts' being referred to as 'sentence stems' in both collaboration and writing. Additionally, there is a psychological theory called 'script theory,' not disimilar to the methods from last weeks readings, where trends in behavior are described as a students' script.

Name: Ariana Ravitch
Comment: I think I would also add a sixth question to this set of guiding questions, which is what could the unintended consequences of a scaffold be? Sometimes, scaffolds have the potential to have adverse effects, so it is important to recognize this possibility to understand how to recognize such effects and mitigate them.

Name: Ariana Ravitch
Comment: I think the idea of a CSCL script is wonderful, but it is also possible that such a scaffold can cause social dynamics to become negative or inflamed. If one student is bullying another student or if one student feels uncomfortable monitoring a student they do not know well, this could cause negative affect and the student to disengage with the lesson.

Name: Ariana Ravitch
Comment: I think I would caution against this sharing of test scores for pre-tests. It might cause the students who do not see that they are 'superior' to disengage and develop a fixed mindset. Of course, a teacher can create an environment where the students with higher scores are in a group with lower scored students, but to provide the students with scoring information would likely cause adverse affectional effects, even if this intended consequence occurs.

Name: Ariana Ravitch
Comment: https://web.english.upenn.edu/~cavitch/pdf-library/Tomkins_Script_Theory.pdf This is a paper about the Psychological script theory, which I used in my undergraduate course on studying personality of political leaders. Script theory in personality psychology, which this article seems to be a subset of, postulates that people's experiences and reaction tendencies solidify as time goes on; they follow these scripts, leading to observable personality traits. I think it is valuable to understand the process of distilling scripts across activities, beyond the classroom. It also might help with the process of identifying your own or your students' scripts.

Name: Ariana Ravitch
Comment: I wonder how a computer can do this? Quality of collaboration seems like a disputed term to me, or, at the very least, a term that is difficult to define in numbers, which is likely happening in the process of computing described here. How would you all measure 'good quality' collaboration?

Name: Ariana Ravitch
Comment: When I was an undergraduate student in my reading processes and assessment course, we were tasked with giving feedback on each other's handwriting. We were scaffolded by being told the feedback must be 3 parts, each being specific and actionable. I imagine that similar guidance, perhaps a bit more in depth, would be valuable for k-12 students as well.

Name: Shijia Liao
Comment: I think this framework is helpful because it makes it easier to see why similar collaborative activities may work differently depending on who provides the support and what outcome is intended. In classroom settings, support can look similar on the surface, but teachers, peers, or tools may shape participation in very different ways

Name: Weishan Zhong
Comment: I think these activities are important engines that drive knowledge building. Argumentation involves the kind of active construction of knowledge that traditional lecturing can't offer. By actively processing claims and counterclaims, learners can build their own model rather than just simply accepting inforamtion. Peer feedback fosters collective responsibility by shifting the role of the 'evaluator' from teacher to the students themselves. When students use tools like rubrics or templates to provide high-quality feedback to one another, they're taking responsibility for the group's collective output.

Name: Weishan Zhong
Comment: I think this internal collaboration scripts that learners have play a huge role on their motivation to participation and engagement. For me, if I perceive a group activity as not useful to my learning, or if I don't receive enough guidance on a complex activity, I would easily lose interest and be unmotivated to participate

Name: Aashna Patel
Comment: Unproductive collaboration patterns waste precious class time and can gradually demotivate students from wanting to collaborate at all. When the same few students keep doing most of the thinking and participation, others may become even more disengaged, which leads to shallow discussion and unequal learning. It also prevents the group from really building on one another’s ideas in a meaningful way. Some strategies I’ve seen help are assigning clear roles, making each person responsible for one full element of the activity, and asking the group to work together to create a shared artifact so that everyone’s contribution is visible. Open communication also matters, because groups need space to address imbalance early. I also think instructor intervention and facilitation are important, especially when discussions become superficial or participation is uneven.

Name: Aashna Patel
Comment: I found it especially useful that scaffolding in collaborative learning can come from different people and groups, not just one source. In my Curriculum Development and Enactment class last semester, scaffolding first came from within our own group as we worked through activities together. Then, when groups shared their work with the class, other groups added another form of scaffolding by giving feedback, asking critical questions, and building on ideas. This showed me that collaborative learning is stronger when there are multiple forms of scaffolding from different people

Name: Aashna Patel
Comment: This point stood out to me because it feels like a very common problem in collaborative learning. It connects back to the paper’s earlier point that learners often have difficulty collaborating productively and may slip into superficial discussion. Peer feedback can easily become forced or shallow when students are not given enough guidance, and sometimes people give comments just for the sake of giving them. I have also seen that students may not always value peer feedback, especially when it is not aligned with a rubric or clear assessment criteria. That is why structured support like rubrics or feedback templates can make such a difference.

Name: Dizhi Zhang
Comment: This section emphasizes the importance of maintaining flexible "scaffolding," particularly through the concept of "fading," which involves gradually reducing support as learners improve. This view is closely related to Vygotsky's "Zone of Proximal Development" (ZPD) theory, as both emphasize providing learners with the appropriate amount of support to maximize efficiency. If support is removed too early or too late, the learning effect is often greatly reduced. The article also points out that the rigid "fading" model, which adjusts support based solely on time or repetition, often has little effect because different learners develop at different paces. This indicates that scaffolding support should be adaptable, making corresponding adjustments according to learners' actual progress. In conclusion, effective collaborative support is not static, but rather, it must continuously and dynamically adjust to the needs of learners. Only in this way can it effectively promote self-regulation in learners.

Name: Stephanie Shin
Comment: To be honest, for a while I thought scaffolding and ZPD were one and the same and would use it interchangeably.

Name: Stephanie Shin
Comment: I totally see your concern! Your comment brings up the point that scaffolds may not always result in the intended outcome. It can be put in place with intentionality, but we can never fully predict student thoughts and processes
